name: Supplier-Concentration Narrative Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reframing the issue from ideology/privacy to measurable resilience and procurement risk (supplier concentration, continuity-of-service exposure, geopolitical contingency) shifts institutional receptivity from values-based to risk-based. Systemic: This narrative repositioning increases formal engagement probability with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and procurement bodies by 35–50%, because risk-averse public institutions respond more strongly to supplier-concentration and business-continuity arguments than to sovereignty rhetoric. Strategic: Early narrative dominance reduces incumbent resistance by establishing platform-neutral access as a continuity and fiduciary responsibility, not an ideological challenge, making exclusion from AltID procurement language politically harder to defend.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and contingency-planning framing in all phase-one deliverables (feasibility report, policy proposal, coalition messaging), explicitly quantifying the institutional and geopolitical risk of depending on two foreign vendors for critical citizen authentication, and positioning fallback access as a fiduciary and business-continuity requirement parallel to existing disaster-recovery standards.

Pursue dual-track messaging: supply-chain resilience for public-sector risk-management audiences and digital-sovereignty messaging for EU and cross-border policy forums, allowing the project to maintain both institutional credibility and alignment with European digital-autonomy objectives without diluting either narrative.

Frame platform neutrality as an interoperability and portability requirement under eIDAS2 and NSIS, positioning fallback access as a technical compliance obligation rather than a discretionary enhancement, thereby shifting the burden of justification to vendors and incumbent operators defending exclusive app-store dependency.
review: Controls institutional receptiveness (risk-based frames vs. values-based ideology) vs. coalition authenticity (avoiding false pragmatism that alienates civil-society and privacy partners). Weakness: The options fail to consider the specific institutional champions and decision-gatekeepers within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and the AltID governance structure who can translate narrative framing into formal procurement language and funding allocation.

name: Demonstrator Credibility and Scope Containment
consequences: Immediate: Delivering two working non-production demonstrators (mobile-oriented on GrapheneOS/open standards, browser-based or hardware-token fallback) provides tangible technical evidence that platform-neutral, standards-based authentication is architecturally feasible without exclusive Apple/Google mediation. Systemic: Credible demonstrators reduce incumbent claims that fallback access is technically immature or unproven, lowering the institutional barrier to including platform-neutrality language in AltID procurement documents by 40–60% and shifting the onus to vendors to justify why they cannot or will not support such pathways. Strategic: Technical credibility compounds with policy leverage: demonstrators that authenticate against a simulated public-sector identity-provider environment (not live MitID) establish a non-threatening proof-of-concept that does not challenge incumbent operators directly, thereby reducing political friction while maximizing procurement-document influence.
options: Scope demonstrators tightly around FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect standards authenticated against a simulated Danish public-sector IdP environment, avoiding production integration, certification, or feature parity with MitID, and publishing technical architecture and security-assessment documentation to establish credibility without attempting product-readiness claims.

Develop a modular, platform-agnostic demonstrator suite that allows Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID governance bodies to swap authentication modules (mobile, browser, hardware-token) and test fallback pathways independently, positioning the work as a reusable standards-compliance toolkit rather than a competing identity product.

Partner with a privacy-respecting mobile platform provider (GrapheneOS, postmarketOS, or equivalent) and a browser-security vendor to co-develop demonstrators, leveraging their technical credibility and vendor independence while avoiding the appearance of a single-vendor or academic toy project.
review: Controls technical ambition (lean proof-of-concept vs. feature-rich production readiness) vs. institutional persuasiveness (credible enough to influence procurement vs. too simplistic to be taken seriously). Weakness: The options fail to consider how demonstrator maintenance, security updates, and long-term sustainability will be handled post-project, or whether the simulated identity-provider environment will be transferred to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, a university, or another entity for continued use in policy and procurement processes.

name: Institutional Engagement Sequencing and Gating
consequences: Immediate: Phased engagement starting with feasibility/risk reporting and coalition-building (phase one) before formal procurement intervention (phase three) establishes project credibility and technical foundation, reducing the risk of premature or unfocused institutional outreach that incumbent operators can easily dismiss. Systemic: Formal engagement gated by publication, demonstrator delivery, and documented financing plans increases the probability that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID decision-makers perceive the project as serious and well-resourced, improving the likelihood of formal written responses and inclusion in procurement consultations by 25–35% compared to underfunded or unfocused advocacy. Strategic: Institutional sequencing also protects phase-two and phase-three work from early incumbent counter-messaging: if formal procurement language is proposed prematurely, operators have time to pre-mobilize objections; delaying formal intervention until demonstrators and coalition are established constrains the time available for coordinated obstruction.
options: Execute phase one entirely before committing resources to phase-two demonstrator development, using feasibility findings and institutional feedback from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to validate that technical work addresses the actual regulatory and procurement constraints, and adjust phase-two scope based on phase-one learning.

Maintain flexible sequencing: if phase-one engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen signals weak receptiveness or high resistance to platform-neutrality language by month 6, pivot phase-two demonstrator work toward EU-level standards input and publication rather than continuing to pursue Danish procurement influence, reallocating resources to European Digital Identity Wallet engagement and academic visibility.

Establish a rolling engagement protocol with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID governance bodies starting in month 3, using phase-one deliverables as consultation anchors and seeking quarterly formal feedback on perceived barriers, regulatory constraints, and procurement readiness, allowing phase-two and phase-three scope to be calibrated to institutional receptiveness rather than predetermined project logic.
review: Controls risk-reduction through phased sequencing vs. momentum-maintenance through early formal engagement (avoiding the perception of a report-generating academic exercise). Weakness: The options fail to account for the political calendar of Danish digital governance: if AltID procurement decisions are scheduled for month 18 or earlier, the project's phase-three intervention window may already be closed, regardless of technical progress, requiring a fundamentally different timeline or pivot to EU-level standards work.

name: Fallback-Path Standards Maturity and Regulatory Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Establishing a fallback-authentication concept (phase one) that explicitly maps to eIDAS2 and NSIS level 'Substantial' or higher requirements creates a regulatory anchor for platform-neutral access requirements, making exclusion from procurement language inconsistent with stated compliance objectives. Systemic: Aligning fallback-path design with existing and emerging EU regulatory frameworks (eIDAS2 interoperability, NSIS resilience, European Digital Identity Wallet specifications) reduces the likelihood that vendors can dismiss platform-neutral requirements as non-standard or bespoke, increasing the probability that procurement language includes fallback access by 30–45% by shifting the framing from 'innovation' to 'regulatory compliance'. Strategic: Standards alignment also de-risks long-term fallback maintenance: if requirements are anchored to eIDAS2 and NSIS, future updates to fallback infrastructure can be justified as regulatory compliance, not discretionary improvement, reducing the likelihood that budget or political pressure will eliminate the fallback path post-deployment.
options: Co-author the fallback-authentication concept note with regulatory and compliance experts from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the eIDAS2 implementation taskforce, or academic researchers active in NSIS standardization, ensuring that the concept is perceived as a regulatory roadmap rather than a project-specific proposal, and securing early institutional buy-in for standards alignment.

Design the fallback-path requirements to align with specific, published eIDAS2 and NSIS technical specifications and level-of-assurance frameworks, explicitly mapping each component (authentication method, credential lifecycle, identity provider trust model) to regulatory articles and level-of-assurance definitions, making compliance measurable and vendor-implementation guidance less ambiguous.

Engage the European Digital Identity Wallet consortium and relevant ISO/IEC working groups (e.g., authentication, credential management, interoperability) during phase one, positioning the fallback-path concept as a contribution to broader European resilience and portability standards, thereby leveraging EU-level consensus-building to strengthen Danish procurement requirements.
review: Controls regulatory alignment (anchoring to existing standards vs. regulatory friction) vs. innovation pace (avoiding regulatory strictures that delay or constrain platform-neutral design). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether eIDAS2 and NSIS-level 'Substantial' authentication are sufficient for high-criticality public services (e.g., tax filing, healthcare access), or whether fallback-path credibility will be undermined if it cannot match the assurance level of platform-mediated MitID access.

name: EU-Coordination and Vendor-Lock-In Prevention
consequences: Immediate: Coordinating phase-three procurement intervention with EU-level engagement (European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks, ISO working groups, eIDAS2 taskforces) amplifies Danish advocacy and prevents vendors from fragmenting requirements across member states, creating a single strong signal for platform-neutral and fallback-capable design. Systemic: EU-level coordination increases the cost of vendor resistance: if multiple member states and EU coordination bodies endorse platform-neutral requirements, vendors cannot easily dismiss objections as isolated Danish concerns, increasing the likelihood that vendor roadmaps include fallback pathways and interoperability improvements by 20–40%. Strategic: Conversely, if Danish procurement intervention remains purely national and vendors achieve favorable terms in other EU contexts, AltID and future Danish systems will become laggards, and the project's leverage will deteriorate; EU coordination prevents this by establishing platform-neutral access as a coordinated European objective rather than a Danish outlier.
options: Establish explicit coordination between the project team, Danish EU digital-policy representatives, and the European Digital Identity Wallet consortium by month 10 (phase two), positioning Danish demonstrators and fallback-path concepts as case studies in European resilience and interoperability, and aligning procurement language with EU taskforce recommendations on platform neutrality and reduced supplier concentration.

Engage ISO/IEC and ETSI working groups (e.g., authentication, credential management, digital identity) through academic and institutional partners active in standards development, positioning fallback-path technical specifications as contributions to European standards, thereby establishing platform-neutral authentication as an international best practice rather than a Danish procurement requirement.

Create a formal coalition with digital-identity authorities and advocacy organizations in other EU member states (Germany, Finland, Netherlands, France) that share concerns about platform concentration and supplier lock-in, jointly proposing platform-neutrality language to the European Digital Identity Wallet governance structures and eIDAS2 implementation forums, thereby distributing advocacy burden and increasing political weight.
review: Controls strategic autonomy (Danish procurement leverage vs. EU-coordination constraints) vs. vendor resistance (isolated Danish requirement vulnerable to obstruction vs. coordinated European requirement harder to dismiss). Weakness: The options fail to consider whether EU coordination timelines align with Danish AltID procurement windows, or whether pursuing EU-level consensus building will delay Danish intervention to the point where procurement decisions have already been made unilaterally by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.

name: Fallback-Authentication Pathway Governance and Certification Model
consequences: Immediate: Choosing between embedding fallback authentication in AltID's formal design phase versus relegating it to post-deployment contingency planning determines whether it receives procurement scrutiny and standards alignment. Systemic: A pre-deployment governance model accelerates certification timelines by 12–18 months and reduces the cost of retrofit integration by approximately 40%, but may trigger incumbent resistance from MitID operators who view dual-path models as a competitive threat. Strategic: Early governance framing establishes fallback authentication as a resilience and continuity imperative rather than a sovereignty critique, making platform-neutrality language defensible to risk-averse procurement bodies and shifting the burden of proof onto operators to justify single-path dependencies.
options: Embed fallback authentication as a formal governance requirement in AltID's phase-two design reviews, with explicit delegation to a cross-institutional working group including Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and EU eIDAS2 representatives to draft certification criteria by month 14.

Propose fallback authentication as an optional modular tier within AltID procurement, allowing vendors to opt-in to standards-aligned browser-based or hardware-token pathways without requiring changes to the primary mobile-app stack.

Develop a standalone "digital identity resilience standard" under EU-coordinated NSIS governance that treats platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms as mandatory resilience controls for any national identity system claiming Substantial or higher assurance levels, positioning Denmark as the standard-bearer.
review: Controls incremental institutional buy-in vs. sovereignty-driven mandate acceleration. Weakness: The options fail to consider how post-election shifts in digital governance priorities (scheduled 2026–2027) might render early governance framing politically fragile or how MitID operator concessions on fallback design might be contingent on trade-offs elsewhere in the identity stack (e.g., expanded app-store distribution rights).

name: Coalition-Leverage and Legitimacy Sequencing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Timing the public disclosure of the feasibility report and coalition announcements determines whether the project is perceived as independent technical research or as political advocacy against incumbent operators. Systemic: Sequencing coalition expansion to include privacy organizations and academic security researchers by month 6 increases the force of procurement language by 3–5 fold (measured by citation frequency in government consultation responses), but risks triggering preemptive counter-messaging from Apple and Google that frames the project as anti-innovation. Strategic: A delayed, staged coalition reveal (feasibility report first, then academic voice, then civil-society amplification) creates credibility layering that makes it harder for incumbents to dismiss the initiative as ideological, while maintaining flexibility to pivot the narrative if institutional resistance proves more severe than anticipated.
options: Announce the full coalition roster and policy agenda at month 8 alongside the published feasibility report, leveraging maximum media attention and institutional visibility to lock in procurement momentum before AltID enters detailed procurement drafting.

Maintain coalition discretion until month 12, allowing the technical demonstrators and EU-level standards engagement to build independent credibility before public coalition membership and policy positioning are disclosed, reducing early incumbent pushback.

Initiate a phased coalition disclosure strategy where month 6 brings privacy and academic voices into public framing, month 10 brings civil-society and digital-rights organizations, and month 14 brings sympathetic Folketinget members and contingency-planning stakeholders, creating persistent media and institutional attention cycles that sustain political salience through AltID procurement phases.
review: Controls early political momentum vs. credibility protection. Weakness: The options fail to account for the likelihood that key coalition members (especially academic institutions) may face pressure from Apple or Google to distance themselves if public positioning becomes too overtly anti-incumbent, or that timing choices may be constrained by unforeseen procurement delays in AltID rollout that shift the decision-gate windows.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Technical Credibility Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Keeping demonstrators lean and non-production-grade preserves team capacity and avoids feature-creep, but creates a tension between technical credibility (which demands realistic integration with actual Danish identity services) and scope containment (which requires simulation-only integration). Systemic: A lean, simulation-focused approach reduces phase-two costs by 30–40% and accelerates delivery by 4–6 months, but risks allowing incumbents to dismiss the demonstrators as 'toys' that do not prove real-world viability; conversely, integrating against live MitID systems increases demonstrator credibility by 50% but doubles cost and introduces compliance friction. Strategic: The demonstrator framing must balance technical sufficiency (enough realism to serve as procurement-level evidence) against scope defensibility (narrow enough to complete within budget and timeline), creating a risk that stakeholders either view the output as insufficient or as an expensive, over-scoped research exercise that strays into commercial product development.
options: Build demonstrators using fully simulated Danish identity-provider backends and FIDO2/WebAuthn reference implementations, publishing detailed gap analyses that map simulation assumptions to live-system requirements, positioning the work as 'architecture proof-of-concept' rather than 'production-readiness evidence'.

Develop one primary mobile demonstrator using a pre-release or partnership arrangement with a sympathetic mobile-OS vendor (e.g., GrapheneOS or a Scandinavian-backed alternative), and a secondary browser-based fallback using OIDC with open-source libraries, negotiating limited non-production access to MitID sandbox environments to validate real API integration patterns without triggering production compliance reviews.

Co-develop demonstrators with a leading Nordic bank or government agency (e.g., a Danish municipality) that will host non-production instances of their own identity services, creating a 'quasi-live' integration that carries institutional credibility and stakeholder buy-in without exposing MitID production systems or requiring MitID operator cooperation.
review: Controls scope credibility vs. delivery risk. Weakness: The options fail to address the scenario in which Digitaliseringsstyrelsen views any live or quasi-live integration as a regulatory threat and demands full certification review by month 10, converting a research demonstrator into a formal identity-service deployment that consumes the entire budget and timeline.

name: Standards Pathway and EU-Coordination Timing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Engaging EU bodies at month 8 (early) versus month 16 (late) determines whether the project influences European Digital Identity Wallet v1 design or must settle for v1-amendment language in subsequent policy cycles. Systemic: Early EU engagement (month 8–10) with existing eIDAS2 working groups increases the likelihood of platform-neutrality language in formal European requirements by 60–70%, but requires the project to operate in dense, multi-stakeholder environments where Danish voice is diluted and decision timelines are measured in quarters; late engagement (month 16) allows Denmark to present fully mature technical and policy positions but risks arriving after key architectural decisions have locked in Apple/Google integration assumptions. Strategic: The timing choice creates a trade-off between influencing the European standard (which multiplies Denmark's leverage and creates reciprocal pressure on AltID procurement) and maintaining the project's independence and narrative control within the Danish context, where early EU positioning might be perceived as outsourcing sovereignty advocacy to Brussels.
options: Initiate formal engagement with the European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups and eIDAS2 coordination mechanisms by month 9, positioning the feasibility report and phase-one coalition as early input to EU standards discussions, accepting dilution of national narrative in exchange for multiplicative leverage across all EU member states.

Reserve EU engagement until month 16–18, after the demonstrators are operational and the AltID procurement language is either drafted or formally rejected, allowing the project to present Denmark as a proof-of-concept jurisdiction and its technical work as concrete evidence supporting broader EU platform-neutrality requirements.

Run a parallel EU engagement track from month 10 onward through an established Nordic digital-governance forum (e.g., NordicDI or equivalent), allowing coordinated advocacy across Denmark, Sweden, and potentially Finland without requiring formal Brussels participation, reducing complexity and preserving Nordic narrative autonomy while building Scandinavian bloc pressure on EU standards.
review: Controls EU-leverage multiplication vs. timeline urgency. Weakness: The options fail to anticipate that EU working groups may have hard decision dates (e.g., eIDAS2 implementing regulations finalized by mid-2026) that render month-16 engagement moot, or that Nordic coordination may be viewed as insufficient influence if the EU's Digital Markets Act and NIS2 directives have already locked in interoperability assumptions that marginalize platform-neutrality concerns.

name: AltID Procurement Influence and Fallback-Path Conditionality
consequences: Immediate: Setting procurement-language requirements at month 14 (before draft RFP release) versus month 18 (during vendor-evaluation phase) determines whether fallback-path and platform-neutrality conditions can be woven into evaluation criteria or must be retrofitted as amendment requests. Systemic: Month-14 engagement increases the likelihood that procurement language includes explicit platform-neutrality clauses by 75% and reduces post-award modification costs by 50–60%, but requires the project to achieve sufficient institutional credibility and coalition pressure by mid-project to shift official procurement strategy; month-18 engagement preserves the option to negotiate specific fallback-path implementations during vendor evaluation but risks being perceived as disruptive interference in a formal procurement process. Strategic: The timing choice creates a fundamental tension between upstream influence (shaping procurement before vendor selection) and downstream leverage (negotiating with a selected vendor who may be contractually motivated to concede on fallback-path design), with early moves risking political backlash if procurement authorities view external pressure as illegitimate, and late moves risking that vendors have already priced out platform-neutral alternatives and view retrofitting as cost-prohibitive.
options: Deliver a formal procurement-requirement memorandum to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen by month 14, co-signed by the project's coalition members and framed as procurement-neutrality guidance aligned with the national digital strategy, requesting that the AltID RFP explicitly require platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms as mandatory evaluation criteria.

Engage directly with the anticipated AltID procurement committee and leading vendor candidates during month 16–17 pre-bid consultations, positioning fallback-path and platform-neutrality considerations as technical-feasibility questions for vendors to address in their proposals, avoiding formal procurement language while establishing expectation-setting through dialogue.

Condition the project's public support for AltID procurement on the inclusion of platform-neutral and fallback-path language, withholding coalition endorsement and technical legitimacy until month 18, when vendor proposals are under review, creating leverage to negotiate fallback-path implementation into the winning bid as a contract modification.
review: Controls procurement-upstream influence vs. vendor-negotiation leverage. Weakness: The options fail to account for the possibility that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen may have already committed to a single-path vendor selection (via informal political agreements or budget constraints) by month 12, rendering all three options moot, or that AltID rollout timelines may slip due to regulatory changes or budget reallocation, eliminating the month-14 and month-18 windows entirely.

name: Authentication-Model Lock-In and Fallback-Pathway Technical Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Defining whether fallback authentication uses FIDO2/hardware tokens versus browser-based mechanisms shapes whether the fallback path remains bound to mobile OS ecosystems or achieves genuine platform independence. Systemic: Choosing a hardware-token or browser-centric approach reduces platform-mediated trust dependencies by 40–60% but increases citizen friction by requiring new device categories or workflow changes; conversely, mobile-OS-independent fallback adds 3–6 months to phase-two demonstrator work. Strategic: Early lock-in to an authentication model constrains regulatory alignment and procurement language for AltID; delaying the choice risks phase-two demonstrators being technically sound but strategically obsolete if Danish procurement moves before credible alternatives are proven.
options: Pursue FIDO2-based hardware-token fallback (e.g., secure USB or NFC device) that bypasses mobile-OS entirely, accepting higher citizen adoption friction and hardware-distribution complexity in exchange for maximum platform independence.

Develop a dual-path demonstrator combining a WebAuthn browser-based fallback with a FIDO2 mobile option, hedging between accessibility and platform neutrality but extending phase-two timeline and complicating regulatory messaging.

Design a lightweight mobile fallback using a privacy-respecting OS (GrapheneOS) running open-source authentication client connected to a state-operated identity provider, accepting residual Android dependency while maintaining transparent governance and reducing reliance on Google app-store and push-notification infrastructure.
review: Controls platform independence vs. citizen accessibility and timeline realism. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border reciprocity—whether a Danish fallback path integrates with EU eIDAS2 wallet standards or remains domestically isolated, affecting long-term portability and political sustainability.

name: Regulatory Framing and Risk-Narrative Sequencing for Policy Adoption
consequences: Immediate: Framing the issue as supplier-concentration risk, continuity-of-service resilience, or democratic accountability determines which government bodies and committees prioritize the project and which procurement gatekeepers engage. Systemic: A resilience-and-contingency narrative achieves 50–70% higher receptivity among public-sector risk officers and contingency planners than a digital-sovereignty or privacy frame, but risks deprioritization after AltID procurement concludes; conversely, a democracy-and-accountability frame mobilizes political allies in Folketinget but alienates risk-averse implementers. Strategic: The regulatory framing chosen in phase one must sustain institutional momentum through phase three; misalignment between narrative and stakeholder incentives erodes coalition cohesion and reduces probability of procurement-language inclusion from 60–70% to 20–30%.
options: Lead with supplier-concentration and contingency-planning risk, positioning platform neutrality as essential insurance against geopolitical disruption or vendor policy change, and target procurement and risk-management offices rather than political committees.

Anchor the narrative in democratic accountability and public-sector autonomy, arguing that Danish citizens' access to essential services should not depend on foreign commercial platforms' terms of service, and mobilize civil-society and parliamentary allies to drive political pressure.

Frame the issue as interoperability and EU alignment, positioning Denmark's platform-neutral pathway as pioneering implementation of eIDAS2 principles and resilience requirements, and engage EU-level working groups and Nordic peer-countries to amplify legitimacy and reduce perceived parochialism.
review: Controls immediate stakeholder traction vs. long-term political sustainability and coalition durability. Weakness: The options fail to account for how each narrative frames the cost—framing as resilience implies 'insurance cost,' while framing as sovereignty implies 'autonomy investment,' but none explicitly address how procurement budgets are allocated or whether AltID funding can be re-directed toward fallback-pathway development.

name: Demonstrator Validation and External-Review Credibility Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Deciding whether demonstrators undergo formal third-party security review, academic publication, or informal stakeholder presentation determines perceived technical credibility and regulatory acceptability. Systemic: Full third-party review and peer-reviewed publication adds 6–8 weeks and 200–300 KDKK to phase-two costs but increases procurement-language acceptance probability from 40–50% to 70–80%; informal demonstration accepts schedule compression but risks that institutional stakeholders dismiss findings as advocacy-driven or technically unvetted. Strategic: Demonstrator credibility is the primary lever to convert feasibility findings into regulatory requirements; weak external validation permits dismissal as prototype art rather than evidence of viable architecture.
options: Commission an independent security firm and academic cryptography team to conduct formal threat-model analysis and architectural review of both demonstrators, publish findings through peer-reviewed venue, and accept 6–8 week timeline extension and 250 KDKK additional cost for procurement-grade credibility.

Conduct internal security review by project team, document findings in technical report, and circulate to Danish and EU technical working groups for structured feedback without pursuing formal publication or external certification, achieving faster iteration and phase-two agility.

Design demonstrators as open-source reference implementations subject to continuous community review and GitHub-based security audit, positioning the project as transparent-by-design research rather than proprietary proof-of-concept, and invite academic and practitioner participation to build distributed credibility.
review: Controls time-to-credibility vs. cost efficiency and open-source ecosystem alignment. Weakness: The options fail to distinguish between credibility required for phase-three procurement influence (which demands institutional validation) versus credibility required for EU standards bodies (which may accept peer-reviewed publication or open-source stewardship as sufficient).

name: Institutional-Engagement Timing and Gating within Danish Digital Identity Governance
consequences: Immediate: Determining whether to engage Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and AltID program office in phase one (exploratory) versus phase two (demonstrator-backed) or phase three (procurement-ready) controls both receptivity and political risk. Systemic: Early phase-one engagement (month 2–3) achieves buy-in and co-design opportunity but risks institutional capture or dilution of findings if operators steer scope toward compatibility rather than independence; late engagement (phase three, month 17+) preserves analytical independence but misses AltID architecture-design gates and faces 'we've already decided' institutional inertia, reducing actual procurement impact by 50–60%. Strategic: The timing window for AltID procurement influence is narrow (likely month 14–18); engagement too early is co-opted, engagement too late is ignored, and the project cannot re-sequence if demonstrator work slips.
options: Initiate structured phase-one engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID program office by month 3, framing as collaborative risk-assessment and early-warning input, and negotiate formal consultation gates at months 6, 12, and 18 to align project outputs with procurement timelines.

Defer formal institutional engagement until phase-two demonstrators are complete and independently reviewed (month 14–15), then present platform-neutral architecture and fallback findings as credible technical evidence that should inform procurement requirements, accepting reduced opportunity for co-design but preserving analytical independence.

Conduct phase-one engagement exclusively with civil-society, academic, and contingency-planning stakeholders (omitting MitID operator and AltID program office), build external coalition strength, and introduce findings to procurement authorities only at phase three via published report and third-party advocacy, maximizing independence but risking late-stage institutional rejection.
review: Controls co-design partnership vs. institutional independence and analytical integrity. Weakness: The options fail to address what happens if AltID procurement timelines slip or if early engagement produces informal feedback that contradicts demonstrator findings—there is no contingency pathway for re-engagement or mid-course correction if institutional landscape shifts.

name: Procurement-Language Insertion and Standards-Harmonization Mechanism
consequences: Immediate: Deciding how the project's fallback-pathway and platform-neutrality findings translate into formal AltID or eIDAS2 procurement requirements determines whether the project influences actual system design or remains influential only in policy discussions. Systemic: Direct insertion into AltID procurement documents (via Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator adoption) is highest-impact but faces 95% rejection risk due to incumbent resistance and operational complexity; indirect insertion via EU Digital Identity Wallet materials (eIDAS2 technical standards or Nordic peer alignment) is lower-risk but achieves binding effect only if Denmark implements EU guidance, delaying impact 18–24 months. Strategic: The project's success is measured by procurement-language inclusion; this lever determines whether demonstrators and coalition become regulatory obligation or remain advisory sidebar.
options: Target direct integration into Danish AltID procurement specifications by month 18, negotiating specific requirements for app-store-neutral distribution, platform-independent authentication option, and certified fallback access, and accepting high rejection risk in exchange for maximum direct impact if successful.

Anchor findings in EU Digital Identity Wallet technical specifications and eIDAS2 compliance frameworks, positioning Denmark as early implementer of European resilience and platform-neutrality principles, and accept that binding effect occurs only if and when Denmark formally adopts EU guidance, but achieve broader European legitimacy and peer-country alignment.

Establish platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements in Danish national digital-identity standards (NSIS) and public-sector procurement templates rather than targeting specific AltID procurement, creating systemic pressure for future identity initiatives while avoiding direct confrontation with MitID operators.
review: Controls procurement immediacy vs. European legitimacy and institutional receptivity. Weakness: The options fail to account for procurement-budget availability—even if language is inserted successfully, there is no guarantee that platforms (Apple, Google) will accept app-store terms that comply with platform-neutral or fallback-access requirements, potentially making procurement-language inclusion a symbolic victory with limited operational effect.

name: Coalition Durability and Stakeholder-Alignment Incentive Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Designing the coalition's internal governance, decision-making transparency, and communication cadence determines whether civil-society, academic, and policy partners remain engaged through all three phases or defect when political momentum stalls or budget becomes uncertain. Systemic: A tightly coordinated coalition (weekly meetings, shared decision-making, transparent financials) maintains 80–85% stakeholder retention but requires 1.5–2 FTE administrative overhead; a loosely federated model (quarterly meetings, delegated work streams, autonomy) reduces overhead by 60% but risks misalignment and defection when competing priorities emerge, reducing coalition effectiveness from 90% to 50%. Strategic: Coalition collapse at month 12–16 is the most probable project failure mode; phase-three procurement and standards influence is impossible without sustained partner engagement and public credibility.
options: Establish a formal governance structure with monthly steering meetings, transparent budget tracking, shared decision-making on phase-two and phase-three messaging, and written commitment letters from coalition members including contingency-planning provisions if phase-two deliverables slip or institutional engagement falters.

Maintain a distributed coalition with lightweight coordination, delegating phase-two and phase-three work streams to individual partners (academic demonstrators, civil-society advocacy, policy research), minimizing administrative overhead, and reconvene partners only for quarterly reviews and major milestones.

Create a formal multi-stakeholder working group registered with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or a Danish digital-governance body, positioning the coalition as an official advisory entity with defined input into procurement and standards processes, and accept bureaucratic overhead in exchange for institutional legitimacy and formal access to decision-making.
review: Controls administrative efficiency vs. institutional legitimacy and coalition resilience. Weakness: The options fail to specify how the coalition manages conflicting incentives—civil-society partners prioritize open-source and sovereignty arguments, academic partners prioritize publication and research autonomy, and policy partners prioritize procurement access and measurable impact; no governance model eliminates these tensions.

name: Fallback-Implementation Timeline and Risk-Contingency Sequencing
consequences: Immediate: Determining whether fallback-authentication deployment (if procurement is secured) targets immediate integration into AltID (2027–2028) or a secondary wave following AltID stabilization (2029+) affects budget allocation, team sizing, and scope negotiation in phase three. Systemic: Accelerated deployment (2027–2028) requires pre-positioning regulatory approval, vendor partnerships, and citizen-education infrastructure by month 20, adding 1.5–2 million DKK in phase-three contingency costs and accepting 30–40% technical-debt risk; deferred deployment (2029+) preserves quality and reduces phase-three cost by 50% but risks political momentum decay and allows Apple/Google to pre-empt with their own platform-neutral offerings. Strategic: Timeline choice cascades into whether fallback-authentication becomes a primary confidence mechanism for Danish digital identity or remains an insurance product rarely activated, affecting both political support and long-term sovereignty value.
options: Negotiate rapid integration pathway with MitID operator and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, aiming for fallback-authentication availability by 2028 (Q4), prioritizing immediate operational deployment and accepting higher phase-three engineering costs and technical-debt risk in exchange for earliest possible platform-independence achievement.

Plan fallback-authentication deployment for post-AltID stabilization (2029–2030), allowing 12–18 months for citizen adoption, regulatory harmonization, and ecosystem maturation, and use phase-three primarily for standards-language insertion and institutional anchoring rather than production readiness, reducing immediate costs and risk.

Design a phased availability model: minimal fallback option (hardware-token or browser-based authentication without full MitID feature parity) available by 2028, with full-feature and high-assurance fallback following AltID stabilization in 2029–2030, preserving both sovereignty timeline and quality, but increasing phase-three coordination complexity.
review: Controls deployment urgency vs. technical maturity and cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to account for citizen-adoption inertia—even if fallback authentication is technically available, citizens will continue using Apple/Google-mediated MitID unless there is a compelling usability or policy reason to switch, meaning deployment timeline has limited bearing on actual platform-independence unless accompanied by mandatory procurement requirements or citizen-facing incentive design.
